medications.drug_name: hydrocortisone 1% cream (mild topical corticosteroid) to itchy spots
medications.indication_rationale: Symptomatic relief for the most pruritic lesions.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply twice daily to itchy spots (as per source note)
10 mg orally daily (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Not indicated for benign self-limiting PR.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take cool/lukewarm (not hot) showers, avoid harsh soaps and heavy sweating, and continue normal activities (it's not contagious).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the temporary itch/redness; reassurance supports normal function.

patient_education_points.point: This is pityriasis rosea — a harmless, non-contagious rash that follows a mild virus. It's not an allergy and you can't pass it on.
It's frustrating but self-limiting over 6–8 weeks and may leave temporary marks that fade. Use the anti-itch measures for comfort.
See a doctor if it spreads to your palms/soles, you get mouth sores, or you feel systemically unwell with a fever — a blood test may then be needed.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; syphilis serology only if palms/soles/mucosa involved or atypical.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Palm/sole involvement, mouth sores, or systemic illness — seek review/serology.

must_include_items: Diagnose pityriasis rosea AFTER screening for secondary syphilis (palms/soles/mucosa/nodes) and a drug eruption
Reassure it is benign, non-contagious and self-limiting
Symptomatic itch control (topical steroid/antihistamine, cool showers)
acceptable_alternatives: Consider syphilis serology if any risk factors
errors_of_commission: Sending benign PR to ED for a 'severe allergy'
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires screened reassurance for benign PR with symptomatic care and a secondary-syphilis safety-net; unscreened reassurance or over-escalation are failures.
